title: Tai Chi
subtitle: Gentle, evidence-based movement for balance, mobility and well-being — built for older and deconditioned users, and scalable for the fit.
disclaimer: Not medical advice. Tai Chi is an adjunct activity, not a treatment or cure for any condition. If you are older, pregnant, deconditioned, or have a medical condition, get clinician clearance (PAR-Q+ screening) before starting. Stop and seek care for chest pain, severe breathlessness, dizziness or faintness, or new or worsening joint pain.

levels.name: Seated / chair
levels.desc: Lowest-impact entry: all movements done seated. Safest start for the most deconditioned users or for symptom days.

levels.name: Supported standing
levels.desc: Standing with a chair or wall for support — builds standing tolerance and confidence before unassisted balance work.

levels.name: Foundational standing
levels.desc: Unassisted standing forms with higher, knee-sparing stances. The balance-and-falls training core.

levels.name: Advanced flows
levels.desc: Longer flows, more repetitions and lower stances for fitter users wanting a greater challenge.

programs.name: Tai Chi for Arthritis (Sun-style)
programs.desc: Sun-style program by Dr Paul Lam (~12 movements: a 6-movement core plus extension). Higher, upright stances and follow-step footwork reduce knee loading and train balance continuously — the safest standing entry point. CDC-recognized for fall prevention.

programs.name: Tai Ji Quan: Moving for Better Balance (8-form)
programs.desc: Dr Fuzhong Li's 8-form core (from the Simplified 24-form) plus mini therapeutic movements. Highest-tier evidence-based falls-prevention program with the strongest single trial in the field.

programs.name: Simplified Yang 24-form
programs.desc: The aspirational longer standing form for fitter users — more demanding flows once balance and lower-limb tolerance are established.

programs.name: Seated adaptations
programs.desc: Chair versions of the above, always available for the most deconditioned users or on symptom days.

benefits.detail: ~20% fewer fallers (RR 0.80) and ~30% lower fall rate; effect strengthens with practice frequency.
Consistent gains in functional tests (timed up-and-go, standing balance) and reduced fear of falling.
Repeated improvement across conditions (osteoarthritis, fibromyalgia and others).
Clinically meaningful symptom improvement; at least as effective as aerobic exercise at 24–52 weeks.
Short-term improvement in pain, stiffness and function; higher-stance Sun-style preferred to limit knee loading.
~6 mmHg systolic / ~4 mmHg diastolic vs inactivity; modest, and not superior to other aerobic exercise.
Six-minute walk distance ~+41 m and better respiratory quality of life.
Reductions in stress/anxiety/depressive symptoms (effect sizes ~0.5–0.66); heterogeneous evidence.
Benefits for executive function and global cognition in mild cognitive impairment; larger with longer practice.
HbA1c ~ -0.5 to -0.7 points; high heterogeneity; adjunct to standard care, not a treatment.
Mixed findings; not reviewed in depth yet. No sleep claims made.
Low energy cost; BMI often unchanged. Supports weight management indirectly but is not a standalone weight-loss method.

not_supported: Tai Chi does not cure or treat any disease; benefits are adjunctive.
It is not a primary weight-loss method (low MET cost; BMI often unchanged).
For blood pressure and several diabetes markers it is not superior to ordinary aerobic exercise — its edge is accessibility, low impact, balance training and adherence.
Cognition, mood and diabetes benefits rest on heterogeneous, modest-quality trials.
Sleep benefit is not yet substantiated.

setup: Supportive footwear (or bare feet on a non-slip surface)
safety.note: Trials across these conditions reported few or no serious adverse events when Tai Chi is taught and scaled appropriately. The default standing style is the higher-stance Sun-style to protect knees and balance.

sessions.note: Guided sessions (movement library, daily rotation, and instructor-reviewed videos) are the next research deliverables. This page ships the evidence foundation that those build on.
planned: 10–13 min daily sessions that rotate to cover the whole body
Longer 20/30/45 min sessions for fitter users
Per-movement instruction with front/side/mirror views and clear cues
